items.q: Why is atmospheric nitrogen unavailable to most plants directly?
items.correct: N2 has a strong triple bond that most organisms cannot break.
items.misconception: Because plants cannot reach high enough to access it.
items.why: Students miss the chemistry. Despite 78% of air being N2, the molecule's triple bond is energetically costly to break. Only specialised nitrogen-fixing bacteria (and lightning, industrial Haber process) can convert it to usable forms.

items.q: What process moves carbon from the atmosphere into plants?
items.why: Students confuse the directions. Photosynthesis takes CO2 OUT of the atmosphere (plants build sugars); respiration RELEASES CO2. Both run simultaneously in plants, but only photosynthesis fixes new carbon.

items.q: What is the difference between in-situ and ex-situ conservation?
items.correct: In-situ protects species in their natural habitat; ex-situ removes them to controlled settings like zoos or seed banks.
items.misconception: They are alternative names for the same approach.
items.random_wrong: One is for plants, the other for animals.
items.why: Students use the terms interchangeably. Each has trade-offs: in-situ preserves ecosystems; ex-situ provides safety net but loses evolutionary context. Most conservation strategies combine both.
The answer examiners want is: Variety of life. “Type of soil” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: Where a species lives. “A species” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many ideas does biodiversity cover (species + within-species + ecosystems)?
items.why: Exact answer: 3. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
